description: This figure presents the iron corner of the ternary Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram at 1150°C with approximately 1 wt% carbon, illustrating the phase boundaries between single-phase austenite and two-phase ferrite-austenite regions. The triangular coordinate system displays manganese content (0–50%) on the horizontal axis and aluminum content (0–30%) on the left axis, with iron as the balance. Curve 1 demarcates alloys exhibiting single-phase γ-solid solution (austenite) structure, while Curve 2 indicates alloys with two-phase structures comprising both γ- and α-solid solutions. The boundary line labeled "ab" separates these distinct phase fields, with data points representing experimentally characterized alloy compositions. This phase diagram serves as a critical guide for designing Fe-Mn-Al alloys (Fermanal) with targeted microstructures, specifically identifying composition ranges (typically 25–35% Mn and 8–12% Al) where stable single-phase austenite can be obtained versus regions where ferrite-austenite duplex structures form upon quenching from 1150°C.

Figure 2 presents optical micrographs of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys demonstrating the effect of manganese content and heat treatment on microstructural evolution. The set comprises four micrographs: images I and II show alloys with 25% Mn and 20% Mn respectively (both containing 10% Al and 1% C), with subfigures (a) representing the quenched condition from 1150°C (×150) and (b) showing the aged condition at 600°C for 50 hours (×500). In the quenched state, the 25% Mn alloy exhibits single-phase austenite (γ), whereas the 20% Mn alloy displays a duplex structure of austenite and ferrite (α). After aging, the 25% Mn alloy reveals dispersed phase precipitation at grain boundaries with enhanced etchability, while the 20% Mn alloy shows retained austenite with acicular morphology surrounding ferrite particles, alongside carbide precipitation at austenite grain boundaries. These microstructural changes correlate with a sharp hardness increase from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37, confirming that aging induces γ-phase decomposition and carbide formation, thereby enabling precipitation hardening in these alloys.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe-27.5Mn-9.1Al-0.92C alloy as a function of aging temperature after 16 hours of heat treatment. The graph contains four distinct curves: ultimate tensile strength (σb), yield strength (σ0.2), elongation (δ), and reduction of area (ψ). The x-axis represents aging temperature ranging approximately from 450°C to 600°C, while the left y-axis indicates strength values in kg/mm² and the right y-axis shows ductility indicators in percentage. As aging temperature increases, both σb and σ0.2 exhibit upward trends, with σb reaching a peak value of approximately 95 kg/mm² at 550°C before declining, whereas σ0.2 reaches around 85 kg/mm² at 550°C. Conversely, the ductility parameters δ and ψ decrease significantly with increasing temperature, reaching minimum values at 550°C (δ approximately 7-8%, ψ approximately 10%) followed by a slight recovery at higher temperatures. These trends demonstrate that 550°C represents the optimal aging temperature for achieving maximum strength, albeit with minimum ductility. Above 550°C, strength decreases and ductility increases due to the coalescence of the dispersed precipitate phase, consistent with the precipitation hardening mechanism described in the accompanying text.
